summary: My biggest takeaways from @EricRies: 1. There’s a force that pulls organizations toward mediocrity and corruption—“financial gravity”: structural pressure from governance. 2. Only 20% of venture-backed founders are still CEO three years post-IPO. 3. Most corporate charters say companies exist to pursue “any lawful activity,” which effectively means maximizing shareholder value. 4. The key question isn’t what protections you need—it’s when. 5. The simplest protection: become a “public benefit corporation.” 6. Anthropic shows how a mission-aligned team and strong governance allow you to move faster. 7. “Harder is easier.”

title: **Deep content post alert** A technical deep dive for your Sunday morning, somewhere between a short...
event_type: Technical deep dive on RLHF numerical instability due to precision mismatch
claims: ["The mechanism of the precision gap causing non-convergence was pinpointed.", "β is not innocent random noise; it is structured and has consistent negative bias."]
core_facts: ["AsyncGRPO was added to the TRL library to decouple inference and training.", "A sanity check with reward = -len and optimal policy = emit EOS immediately did not converge.", "Training forward pass runs in FP32 while inference engine (vLLM) runs in BF16.", "The importance sampling ratio log r = α + β, where α is true policy change and β is precision gap.", "β is small at token level (O(1e-2–1e-1)) but structured, persistent, and worse for low-probability tokens.", "Disabling clipping leads to stable convergence."]
summary: Thomas Wolf reports that AsyncGRPO in TRL does not converge when training forward pass is FP32 and inference engine (vLLM) is BF16 due to a persistent precision gap term in the importance sampling ratio.
